title: Non-canonical dictionaries
learningObjectives: Explain non-canonical dictionaries in plain language
Write down the objective and identify its assumptions
Design one test that could falsify an over-strong interpretation

whyThisLesson.text: Different training seeds can find different sparse dictionaries with similar reconstruction and sparsity, so an SAE basis should not be treated as the unique ground truth. This lesson gives you a concrete test for that claim before the course moves to the next abstraction.

intuition.analogy: A dictionary is like a map drawn at a chosen zoom level. Zooming in can split one region into many roads; zooming out can merge distinct places. Different training seeds can find different sparse dictionaries with similar reconstruction and sparsity, so an SAE basis should not be treated as the unique ground truth.
intuition.analogyLimit: A feature inventory is resolution-dependent. A label can be useful without being an atomic or canonical unit.
intuition.text: Different training seeds can find different sparse dictionaries with similar reconstruction and sparsity, so an SAE basis should not be treated as the unique ground truth.

toyExample.description: Train dictionaries at two widths on the same synthetic factors and inspect when one factor merges, splits, or changes identity across seeds.

steps.result: Specify exactly what non-canonical dictionaries should recover, preserve, or ignore.
steps.insight: The intended factor must be known before a toy result can be judged.

steps.result: Use the same data, capacity, training budget, and evaluation examples for both methods.
steps.insight: Otherwise a visible gain may be a resource difference in disguise.

steps.result: Vary the source correlation, noise, view quality, or sparsity and record the first failure.
steps.insight: The boundary of success is usually more useful than the headline score.

visual.question: Which assumption behind non-canonical dictionaries changes the result most?
visual.description: Move one control at a time. Treat the display as a toy model, then write down the real experiment it suggests.

technical.text: Different training seeds can find different sparse dictionaries with similar reconstruction and sparsity, so an SAE basis should not be treated as the unique ground truth. In practice, write down the input unit, the operation applied to it, and the exact statistic used to score the result. Those are different things. A learned representation can be useful even when the interpretation attached to it turns out to be wrong.

mathematics.explanation: For this lesson, the expression makes the central trade-off explicit. Different training seeds can find different sparse dictionaries with similar reconstruction and sparsity, so an SAE basis should not be treated as the unique ground truth.
mathematics.whyNeeded: It says precisely what the method is being rewarded for.
mathematics.ifRemoved: You could not tell whether two implementations were solving the same problem.
mathematics.encourages: A testable prediction about the chosen objective.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: A feature inventory is resolution-dependent. A label can be useful without being an atomic or canonical unit.

methodComparison.question: What changes with capacity or seed?
methodComparison.practicalImplication: None of these can be diagnosed from a single run.

evidence.claim: Different training seeds can find different sparse dictionaries with similar reconstruction and sparsity, so an SAE basis should not be treated as the unique ground truth.
evidence.note: Course explanation. Use the research library's primary sources for paper-specific empirical claims.

failureModes.name: A score that answers the wrong question
failureModes.mechanism: The objective rewards a proxy while the paper describes a stronger scientific property.
failureModes.symptom: The headline metric rises, but seed stability, controls, or interventions disagree.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Add a matched negative control and one counterfactual intervention.
failureModes.mitigation: Either narrow the claim or add the missing measurement.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Report both the intended benefit and the most plausible shortcut for every SetConCA loss term.

setconcaConnection.relevance: Non-canonical dictionaries determines how SetConCA should represent, group, or evaluate activation views.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The choice of sparse code, view set, coordination loss, or evaluation.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Match the pointwise SAE and SetConCA on model, activation site, width, sparsity budget, and compute.
setconcaConnection.whichFailure: Mistaking stronger agreement for recovery of the intended concept.
setconcaConnection.whichExperiment: Compare fidelity, feature stability, selectivity, and controlled interventions; report each result separately.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: An apparent gain may come from extra context, effective capacity, or easier positives rather than set coordination.

exercises.prompt: A paper claims that non-canonical dictionaries improved interpretability. What one result would you ask for before accepting that claim?
exercises.reveal: Ask for the smallest missing control: a matched baseline, several seeds, a negative control, or an intervention that tests the claimed mechanism.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Explain non-canonical dictionaries to a colleague, then name one thing the method cannot establish by itself.

nextLesson.bridge: Next: Automated interpretability and metric validity. The next idea reuses this lesson's representation and makes a new assumption explicit.

fields.label: Falsifiable claim
fields.prompt: Write the smallest claim this lesson supports.

fields.label: Control
fields.prompt: What control would rule out a shortcut?
